# data/ — the organized sample dataset

This folder holds the document's **Sample Inventory** and the hierarchical
**Specimen → Piece → ROI** organization of the scans, generated by:

```bash
conda run -n hsi python -m hsi_workflow.run_organize
```

## Layout

```
samples.csv               # sample database: one row per physical piece
                          #   (sample_id, material, dataset, source_scan,
                          #    height/width/area px, n_rois, notes)
inventory_summary.json    # metrics: sample counts, sizes, imaging area
manifest.json             # raw-scan + calibration paths, dataset -> tree map
organized/<dataset>/<piece_id>/
    <piece_id>.hdr/.img   # cropped piece cube (calibrated reflectance, ENVI)
    <piece_id>_mask.npy   # which pixels are the fragment
    meta.json             # material, bbox-in-scan, shape, counts
    roi_index.csv         # one row per ROI (bbox, coverage, variance, reflectance)
    rois/<roi_id>.hdr     # cropped ROI sub-cubes
```

## Where the raw data lives

The raw acquisitions (multi-piece dish scans + white/dark calibration cubes,
~21 GB) are **not** in the repo; they stay at the acquisition archive referenced
by `hsi_workflow/config.py` (`_HSI_ROOT`, currently the OneDrive `Summer/hsi`
folder). `manifest.json` records the exact raw paths each organized tree came
from. Cube binaries (`*.img`, `*.bip`, `*.bil`, `*.npy`) are git-ignored; the
small text artifacts (`samples.csv`, manifests, `roi_index.csv`) are tracked.

Fill the `notes` column of `samples.csv` by hand as you learn things about
individual pieces (scratches, known processing differences, orientation, ...) —
it is the document's ID | Material | Dimensions | **Notes** table.
